entries.name: Hohensalzburg Fortress
entries.desc: A hilltop fortress overlooking Salzburg, built by the city's prince-archbishops. One of the largest fully preserved medieval castles in Europe, it was never taken by force.

photo.alt: Hohensalzburg Fortress on its hill above Salzburg
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hohensalzburg-fortress.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Aerial image of Hohensalzburg Fortress (view from the southwest)" by Carsten Steger · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aerial_image_of_Hohensalzburg_Fortress_(view_from_the_southwest).jpg -->

entries.name: Schönbrunn Palace
entries.desc: The Baroque summer residence of the Habsburg emperors, set amid vast formal gardens. Its many rooms and grounds make it one of Vienna's most-visited cultural landmarks.

photo.alt: Schönbrunn Palace facade and gardens in Vienna
photo.credit: <!-- photo: schonbrunn-palace.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Schönbrunn Palace, Vienna.JPG" by Gveret Tered · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sch%C3%B6nbrunn_Palace,_Vienna.JPG -->

entries.name: Gravensteen
entries.desc: A moated stone castle in the heart of Ghent, once the seat of the Counts of Flanders. Restored around 1900, it preserves ramparts, a keep, and displays of medieval weaponry.

photo.alt: Gravensteen castle and moat in Ghent
photo.credit: <!-- photo: gravensteen-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "Gravensteen, Ghent (DSCF0191)" by Trougnouf (Benoit Brummer) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gravensteen,_Ghent_(DSCF0191).jpg -->

entries.name: Château Frontenac
entries.desc: A grand château-style railway hotel overlooking the St. Lawrence River, built by the Canadian Pacific Railway. Its turreted profile dominates the skyline of Old Quebec.

photo.alt: Château Frontenac overlooking the St. Lawrence River in Quebec City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chateau-frontenac.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Chateau Frontenac Quebec City" by Michel Rathwell from Cornwall, Canada · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Chateau_Frontenac_Quebec_City_(25449464907).jpg -->

entries.name: Lovrijenac Fortress
entries.desc: A fortress rising on a sheer rock outside Dubrovnik's western walls, long defending the city from land and sea attack. It gained wider fame as a Game of Thrones filming location.

photo.alt: Lovrijenac Fortress on the cliffs outside Dubrovnik's walls
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lovrijenac-fortress.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Fort Lovrijenac In Dubrovnik" by Sumitsurai · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fort_Lovrijenac_In_Dubrovnik.jpg -->

entries.name: Karlštejn Castle
entries.desc: A hilltop Gothic castle founded by Holy Roman Emperor Charles IV to safeguard the imperial regalia and holy relics. Its Chapel of the Holy Cross is richly decorated with gilding and gemstones.

photo.alt: Karlštejn Castle on its hilltop in the Czech Republic
photo.credit: <!-- photo: karlstejn-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Karlštejn Castle, Czech Republic" by Ввласенко · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Karl%C5%A1tejn_Castle,_Czech_Republic.JPG -->

entries.name: Prague Castle
entries.desc: One of the largest ancient castle complexes in the world, it has served as the seat of Bohemian kings and Czech heads of state. St. Vitus Cathedral rises at its centre.

photo.alt: Prague Castle complex with St. Vitus Cathedral spires above the red rooftops of the city
photo.credit: <!-- photo: prague-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Prague Castle from Charles Bridge panorama.JPG" by Janmad · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Prague_Castle_from_Charles_Bridge_panorama.JPG -->

entries.name: Frederiksborg Castle
entries.desc: A grand Renaissance palace built across three islets in a lake for King Christian IV. Rebuilt after an 1859 fire, it now houses the Museum of National History.

photo.alt: Frederiksborg Castle's red-brick Renaissance towers reflected in the surrounding lake
photo.credit: <!-- photo: frederiksborg-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Frederiksborg slot 2010 a.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Frederiksborg_slot_2010_a.jpg -->

entries.name: Kronborg Castle
entries.desc: A Renaissance fortress guarding the narrow sound between Denmark and Sweden. It is world-famous as Elsinore, the setting of Shakespeare's play Hamlet.

photo.alt: Kronborg Castle's fortified walls, moat, and spired tower guarding the Øresund sound
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kronborg-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Kronborg castle, Helsingør, Denmark.JPG" by Pudelek · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kronborg_castle,_Helsing%C3%B8r,_Denmark.JPG -->

entries.name: Citadel of Saladin
entries.desc: A medieval Islamic fortification begun by Saladin to defend Cairo. It dominated the city for centuries and is crowned by the 19th-century alabaster Mosque of Muhammad Ali.

photo.alt: The Citadel of Cairo crowned by the domes and minarets of the alabaster Mosque of Muhammad Ali
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cairo-citadel.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Citadel of Saladin in Cairo.jpg" by Mohamed3171979 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Citadel_of_Saladin_in_Cairo.jpg -->

entries.name: Fasil Ghebbi
entries.desc: A walled royal compound of castles and palaces built by Ethiopian emperors from the 17th century. Its stone fortresses blend Ethiopian, Indian, and European influences.

photo.alt: Fasilides Palace, the stone castle at the heart of the Fasil Ghebbi royal compound in Gondar
photo.credit: <!-- photo: fasil-ghebbi.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Fasilides Palace 01.jpg" by Bernard Gagnon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fasilides_Palace_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Olavinlinna
entries.desc: A three-tower medieval castle built on a rocky islet in the Finnish lake district to guard the eastern border. The best-preserved medieval fortress in Northern Europe and home to a celebrated annual opera festival held within its walls.

photo.alt: Olavinlinna's three stone towers rising from a rocky islet, reflected in the still lake water
photo.credit: <!-- photo: olavinlinna-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Olavinlinna Savonlinna Finland.jpg" by HENKKA5 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Olavinlinna_Savonlinna_Finland.jpg -->

entries.name: Cité de Carcassonne
entries.desc: A double-walled fortified town in southern France with roots in Roman and medieval defences, heavily restored in the 19th century. Its ring of towers is a landmark of military architecture.

photo.alt: The double walls and turreted towers of the fortified medieval town of Carcassonne
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cite-de-carcassonne.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "Carcassonne - la Cité.jpg" by Petitemontagnedujura · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Carcassonne_-_la_Cit%C3%A9.jpg -->

entries.name: Château de Chenonceau
entries.desc: A Renaissance château famed for its gallery arching across the River Cher on a series of stone bridges. Shaped by a succession of women, it is nicknamed the Château des Dames.

photo.alt: Château de Chenonceau's west facade spanning the River Cher
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chateau-de-chenonceau.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.5 · "Château de Chenonceau - west facade over Cher (4 May 2006).JPG" by Luke van Grieken · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Chenonceau_-_west_facade_over_Cher_(4_May_2006).JPG -->

entries.name: Château d'If
entries.desc: An island fortress in the bay of Marseille, later used as a notorious state prison. It is best known as a setting in Alexandre Dumas's novel The Count of Monte Cristo.

photo.alt: Château d'If island fortress in the bay of Marseille
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chateau-d-if.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Château d'If (Marseille).JPG" by Philippe Alès · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ch%C3%A2teau_d%27If_(Marseille).JPG -->

entries.name: Château de Fontainebleau
entries.desc: A sprawling royal palace used by French monarchs from the Middle Ages to Napoleon, reflecting centuries of additions. Its Renaissance interiors and gallery are especially renowned.

photo.alt: Château de Fontainebleau exterior facade
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chateau-de-fontainebleau.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Chateau de Fontainebleau FRA 007.JPG" by ignis · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Chateau_de_Fontainebleau_FRA_007.JPG -->

entries.name: Mont Saint-Michel
entries.desc: A fortified island abbey rising from tidal flats off the Normandy coast, crowned by a medieval Benedictine monastery. Dramatic tides isolate it from the mainland at high water.

photo.alt: Mont Saint-Michel tidal island with its Gothic abbey rising above the ramparts
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mont-saint-michel.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Mont St Michel in the afternoon.jpg" by Lynx1211 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mont_St_Michel_in_the_afternoon.jpg -->

entries.name: Château de Versailles
entries.desc: The lavish royal residence of Louis XIV and later French kings, a defining symbol of absolute monarchy. It is famed for its Hall of Mirrors and formal gardens.

photo.alt: Palace of Versailles front facade
photo.credit: <!-- photo: palace-of-versailles.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Front of the Palace of Versailles.jpg" by DiscoA340 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Front_of_the_Palace_of_Versailles.jpg -->

entries.name: Château de Villandry
entries.desc: One of the last great Renaissance châteaux of the Loire Valley, best known for its meticulously reconstructed geometric gardens, including an ornamental vegetable garden.

photo.alt: Aerial view of Château de Villandry and its geometric gardens
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chateau-de-villandry-gardens.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Aerial image of Château de Villandry (view from the southeast).jpg" by Carsten Steger · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aerial_image_of_Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Villandry_(view_from_the_southeast).jpg -->

entries.name: Burg Eltz
entries.desc: A medieval castle nestled in a forested valley near the Moselle, never destroyed and owned by the same family for over 800 years. Its clustered towers make it a picture-book fortress.

photo.alt: Burg Eltz castle in its forested Moselle valley
photo.credit: <!-- photo: burg-eltz.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Burg Eltz - Wierschem - Deutschland (51096940091).jpg" by Frans Berkelaar · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Burg_Eltz_-_Wierschem_-_Deutschland_(51096940091).jpg -->

entries.name: Heidelberg Castle
entries.desc: A partly ruined Renaissance palace of the Electors Palatine, set on a hillside above the old town. Its red-sandstone ruins are a celebrated icon of German Romanticism.

photo.alt: Heidelberg Castle ruins above the old town
photo.credit: <!-- photo: heidelberg-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Heidelberg Castle, 2014.JPG" by Bahnfrend · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Heidelberg_Castle,_2014.JPG -->

entries.name: Hohenzollern Castle
entries.desc: The ancestral seat of the Hohenzollern dynasty, which produced Prussian kings and German emperors. The present Neo-Gothic structure crowns a solitary hilltop in the Swabian Alps.

photo.alt: Hohenzollern Castle on its hilltop with a rainbow, Bisingen, Germany
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hohenzollern-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 DE · "Burg Hohenzollern - 6620-2.jpg" by Steschke · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Burg_Hohenzollern_-_6620-2.jpg -->

entries.name: Neuschwanstein Castle
entries.desc: A Romantic-era palace commissioned by King Ludwig II of Bavaria and left unfinished at his death. Its fairy-tale silhouette inspired Disney's Sleeping Beauty castle.

photo.alt: Neuschwanstein Castle against the Bavarian mountainside, Schwangau, Germany
photo.credit: <!-- photo: neuschwanstein-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Neuschwanstein Castle 2024.jpg" by Wilfredor · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Neuschwanstein_Castle_2024.jpg -->

entries.name: Cape Coast Castle
entries.desc: A coastal fort that became a major hub of the transatlantic slave trade. Its dungeons and Door of No Return make it a solemn memorial to the millions shipped from West Africa.

photo.alt: Cannons along the seaside ramparts of Cape Coast Castle, Ghana
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cape-coast-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Cape Coast Castle, Cape Coast, Ghana.JPG" by Rjruiziii · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cape_Coast_Castle,_Cape_Coast,_Ghana.JPG -->

entries.name: Buda Castle
entries.desc: The historic palace of the Hungarian kings on Castle Hill above the Danube. Repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt, it now houses national museums and the Hungarian National Gallery.

photo.alt: Buda Castle illuminated at night above the Danube, Budapest
photo.credit: <!-- photo: buda-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Budapest - Castillo de Buda - Buda Castle - 05.jpg" by Xosema · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Budapest_-_Castillo_de_Buda_-_Buda_Castle_-_05.jpg -->

entries.name: Agra Fort
entries.desc: A walled Mughal fortress-city in red sandstone rebuilt under Emperor Akbar, later enriched with marble palaces. It overlooks the Yamuna River and the nearby Taj Mahal.

photo.alt: Red sandstone ramparts and bastion of Agra Fort, India
photo.credit: <!-- photo: agra-fort.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Agra Fort.01.JPG" by Ashish3724 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Agra_Fort.01.JPG -->

entries.name: Amber Fort
entries.desc: A hilltop fort-palace of the Rajput rulers of Amber, built in pale sandstone and marble. It is celebrated for its terraced courtyards and mirror-inlaid Sheesh Mahal.

photo.alt: Amber Fort on the hillside above Maota Lake, Jaipur, India
photo.credit: <!-- photo: amber-fort.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Amber Fort on the hills of Jaipur.JPG" by Mittalshivam81 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Amber_Fort_on_the_hills_of_Jaipur.JPG -->

entries.name: Mehrangarh Fort
entries.desc: A massive hilltop fort of the Rathore rulers, rising on a cliff high above Jodhpur. Its ramparts overlook the blue-painted houses of the old city and enclose ornate palaces.

photo.alt: Mehrangarh Fort rising on its cliff above Jodhpur, India
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mehrangarh-fort.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Mehrangarh Fort, Jodhpur, India.JPG" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mehrangarh_Fort,_Jodhpur,_India.JPG -->

entries.name: Red Fort
entries.desc: The red-sandstone fortress-palace of the Mughal emperors in Delhi, built by Shah Jahan. A symbol of the empire, it is where India's prime minister addresses the nation each Independence Day.

photo.alt: Ramparts and chhatris of the Red Fort with the national flag, Delhi, India
photo.credit: <!-- photo: red-fort.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Lahori Gate Ramparts - Red Fort - Delhi 2014-05-13 3141.JPG" by Biswarup Ganguly · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lahori_Gate_Ramparts_-_Red_Fort_-_Delhi_2014-05-13_3141.JPG -->

entries.name: Dublin Castle
entries.desc: For centuries the seat of British rule in Ireland, now a government complex used for state ceremonies. It combines a medieval tower with grand Georgian state apartments.

photo.alt: Dublin Castle's Bedford Tower and upper courtyard
photo.credit: <!-- photo: dublin-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Dublin castle bedford tower.JPG" by Velvet · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Dublin_castle_bedford_tower.JPG -->

entries.name: Kilkenny Castle
entries.desc: An Anglo-Norman stronghold that was long the seat of the powerful Butler family. Remodelled over the centuries, it now anchors extensive public parkland in the city centre.

photo.alt: Kilkenny Castle's exterior facade
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kilkenny-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Kilkenny Castle 26-09-2015.JPG" by Normek82 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kilkenny_Castle_26-09-2015.JPG -->

entries.name: Castel del Monte
entries.desc: A 13th-century citadel built by Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II, remarkable for its strict octagonal plan with an octagonal tower at each corner. Its exact purpose remains debated.

photo.alt: Castel del Monte's octagonal fortress in Andria
photo.credit: <!-- photo: castel-del-monte.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Castel del Monte, Andria.jpg" by Luca Lombardi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castel_del_Monte,_Andria.jpg -->

entries.name: Castel Sant'Angelo
entries.desc: A towering cylindrical monument on the Tiber, begun as the mausoleum of Emperor Hadrian and later converted into a papal fortress and prison, linked to the Vatican by a fortified passage.

photo.alt: Castel Sant'Angelo on the Tiber in Rome
photo.credit: <!-- photo: castel-santangelo.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Castel Sant'Angelo a Roma.jpg" by TeKappa · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castel_Sant%27Angelo_a_Roma.jpg -->

entries.name: Castello Sforzesco
entries.desc: A large brick fortress rebuilt by the Sforza dukes of Milan on earlier Visconti foundations. It now holds several city museums, including works linked to Leonardo and Michelangelo.

photo.alt: Castello Sforzesco's brick facade in Milan
photo.credit: <!-- photo: castello-sforzesco.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Castello Sforzesco (Milan) - Facade.jpg" by Pilgab · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castello_Sforzesco_(Milan)_-_Facade.jpg -->

entries.name: Himeji Castle
entries.desc: Japan's finest surviving feudal castle, nicknamed the White Heron for its brilliant white plaster. Its interlocking defensive walls and gates have survived intact for centuries.

photo.alt: White plastered walls and layered roofs of Himeji Castle on its hilltop
photo.credit: <!-- photo: himeji-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Himeji Castle 0804 1.jpg" by Gorgo · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Himeji_Castle_0804_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Kumamoto Castle
entries.desc: A large hilltop castle built by lord Kato Kiyomasa, famed for its steeply curved stone walls designed to repel attackers. It is counted among Japan's three great castles.

photo.alt: Kumamoto Castle's keep and curved stone walls
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kumamoto-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.5 · "Kumamoto Castle 05n3200.jpg" by 663highland · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kumamoto_Castle_05n3200.jpg -->

entries.name: Matsumoto Castle
entries.desc: One of Japan's oldest surviving wooden keeps, nicknamed the Crow Castle for its black exterior. It is a flatland castle ringed by a moat against a backdrop of the Japanese Alps.

photo.alt: Matsumoto Castle's keep reflected in its moat
photo.credit: <!-- photo: matsumoto-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.5 · "130608 Matsumoto Castle Matsumoto Nagano pref Japan02bs4.jpg" by 663highland · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:130608_Matsumoto_Castle_Matsumoto_Nagano_pref_Japan02bs4.jpg -->

entries.name: Nijo Castle
entries.desc: The Kyoto palace of the Tokugawa shoguns, encircled by stone walls and a broad moat. Its rooms feature elaborately painted sliding screens, and its corridors have intentionally squeaky floors designed to detect intruders.

photo.alt: Ninomaru Palace building at Nijo Castle, Kyoto
photo.credit: <!-- photo: nijo-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.5 · "Nijo Castle.jpg" by Keith Pomakis · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Nijo_Castle.jpg -->

entries.name: Osaka Castle
entries.desc: A landmark castle first built by warlord Toyotomi Hideyoshi, set on immense stone walls and a broad moat. The present main keep is a 20th-century reconstruction housing a museum.

photo.alt: Osaka Castle main keep amid cherry blossoms
photo.credit: <!-- photo: osaka-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Osaka castle tenshu.jpg" by Brandon1122334455 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Osaka_castle_tenshu.jpg -->

entries.name: Trakai Island Castle
entries.desc: A Gothic brick castle set on an island in Lake Galvė, a former seat of the grand dukes of Lithuania. Restored in the 20th century, it is reached by footbridges across the water.

photo.alt: Trakai Island Castle on Lake Galvė, Lithuania
photo.credit: <!-- photo: trakai-island-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Trakai Island Castle, Lithuania - Diliff.jpg" by Diliff · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Trakai_Island_Castle,_Lithuania_-_Diliff.jpg -->

entries.name: Vianden Castle
entries.desc: A large Romanesque and Gothic palace of the counts of Vianden, perched above the Our River valley. Sold off and stripped in the 19th century, it was returned to the Luxembourg state and meticulously restored over three decades.

photo.alt: Vianden Castle above the Our River valley
photo.credit: <!-- photo: vianden-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Vianden Castle in Luxembourg - 51129121738.jpg" by TimeTravelRome · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Vianden_Castle_in_Luxembourg_-_51129121738.jpg -->

entries.name: Chapultepec Castle
entries.desc: A hilltop castle in Mexico City's Chapultepec park, later the residence of Emperor Maximilian I and Mexican presidents. It is the only true castle in North America to have housed sovereigns.

photo.alt: Chapultepec Castle overlooking Mexico City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chapultepec-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Castillo de Chapultepec (Museo Nacional de Historia).JPG" by Arturo Chavez · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castillo_de_Chapultepec_(Museo_Nacional_de_Historia).JPG -->

entries.name: Aït Benhaddou
entries.desc: A fortified earthen village of clustered kasbahs along a former caravan route at the edge of the Sahara. Its rammed-earth towers have featured in numerous films and series.

photo.alt: Fortified kasbahs of Aït Benhaddou, Morocco
photo.credit: <!-- photo: ait-benhaddou.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Ksar Aït Benhaddou, Marocco (أيت بن حدو، المغرب, ⴰⵢⵜ ⵃⴰⴷⴷⵓ).jpg" by Petar Milošević · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ksar_A%C3%AFt_Benhaddou,_Marocco_(%D8%A3%D9%8A%D8%AA_%D8%A8%D9%86_%D8%AD%D8%AF%D9%88%D8%8C_%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%85%D8%BA%D8%B1%D8%A8,_%E2%B4%B0%E2%B5%A2%E2%B5%9C_%E2%B5%83%E2%B4%B0%E2%B4%B7%E2%B4%B7%E2%B5%93).jpg -->

entries.name: Muiderslot
entries.desc: A compact square castle with a round tower at each corner, encircled by a water moat near Amsterdam. Built by Count Floris V, it is the most complete and best-preserved medieval castle in the Netherlands.

photo.alt: Muiderslot castle and moat, Netherlands
photo.credit: <!-- photo: muiderslot.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "The Muiderslot.jpg" by Ludovic Hirlimann · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_Muiderslot.jpg -->

entries.name: Malbork Castle
entries.desc: A vast brick fortress built by the Teutonic Knights as their headquarters. It is considered the largest castle in the world by land area and a masterpiece of Gothic brickwork.

photo.alt: Malbork Castle brick fortress walls, Poland
photo.credit: <!-- photo: malbork-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Malbork Castle 2023 016.jpg" by Scotch Mist · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Malbork_Castle_2023_016.jpg -->

entries.name: Castelo de São Jorge
entries.desc: A Moorish-era hilltop castle overlooking Lisbon and the Tagus River. It later became a royal residence and today offers some of the widest panoramic views over the city.

photo.alt: Castelo de São Jorge hilltop walls above Lisbon
photo.credit: <!-- photo: castelo-sao-jorge.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Castelo São Jorge Lisboa.JPG" by LeRoc · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castelo_S%C3%A3o_Jorge_Lisboa.JPG -->

entries.name: Pena Palace
entries.desc: A vividly coloured Romanticist palace built for the Portuguese royal family on a wooded hilltop above Sintra. It blends Gothic, Moorish, and Renaissance revival styles.

photo.alt: Pena Palace colourful towers above the Sintra hillside
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pena-palace.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Pena Palace Sintra.jpg" by Sergey Pesterev · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pena_Palace_Sintra.jpg -->

entries.name: Bran Castle
entries.desc: A hilltop fortress guarding a Transylvanian mountain pass, widely marketed as the inspiration for the home of Bram Stoker's fictional Count Dracula.

photo.alt: Bran Castle rising above the Transylvanian mountain pass
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bran-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Bran Castle (28330078020).jpg" by Gary Todd · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bran_Castle_(28330078020).jpg -->

entries.name: Corvin Castle
entries.desc: A large Gothic-Renaissance castle built by the Hunyadi family, reached across a long timber bridge over a ravine. It is one of the biggest castles in Europe.

photo.alt: Corvin Castle and its timber bridge over the ravine in Hunedoara
photo.credit: <!-- photo: corvin-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Castelul Corvinilor, municipiul Hunedoara 04.JPG" by Munteanu Anca · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castelul_Corvinilor,_municipiul_Hunedoara_04.JPG -->

entries.name: Peleș Castle
entries.desc: A Neo-Renaissance palace in the Carpathian Mountains, built as a summer residence for Romania's first king, Carol I. It was among the first European castles fully wired for electricity.

photo.alt: Peleș Castle in the Carpathian Mountains near Sinaia
photo.credit: <!-- photo: peles-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 RO · "Castelul Peles - Sinaia - Romania.JPG" by Geluxc · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castelul_Peles_-_Sinaia_-_Romania.JPG -->

entries.name: Bojnice Castle
entries.desc: A Romanticist castle rebuilt from a medieval core for nobleman Ján Pálffy, inspired by French châteaux. Its turrets, crenellations, and deep well make it the most visited castle in Slovakia and one of the most photographed in Central Europe.

photo.alt: Bojnice Castle turrets rebuilt in French château style
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bojnice-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Bojnice Castle Slovakia.jpg" by Studiowacho · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bojnice_Castle_Slovakia.jpg -->

entries.name: Spiš Castle
entries.desc: A vast hilltop ruin in eastern Slovakia, one of the largest castle complexes by area in Central Europe. Its whitewashed walls crown a volcanic crag visible for many kilometres across the Spiš plain.

photo.alt: Spiš Castle ruins crowning the crag above the Spiš plain
photo.credit: <!-- photo: spis-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Spiš Castle 39.jpg" by János Korom Dr. · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Spi%C5%A1_Castle_39.jpg -->

meta: Largest by Area in Central Europe

entries.name: Predjama Castle
entries.desc: A Renaissance fortress dramatically built into the mouth of a cliff cave, with a network of natural tunnels behind it. It is often called the largest cave castle in the world.

photo.alt: Predjama Castle built into the mouth of a cliff cave
photo.credit: <!-- photo: predjama-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "El Castillo de Predjama.JPG" by Mariposa.que.vuela · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:El_Castillo_de_Predjama.JPG -->

entries.name: Gyeongbokgung Palace
entries.desc: The main royal palace of the Joseon dynasty in Seoul, destroyed and rebuilt over the centuries. Its restored halls and gates host a daily changing-of-the-guard ceremony.

photo.alt: Gyeonghoeru Pavilion at Gyeongbokgung Palace, reflected in its pond
photo.credit: <!-- photo: gyeongbokgung-palace.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Gyeonghoeru Pavilion in Gyeongbokgung Palace.jpg" by Hhk1201 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gyeonghoeru_Pavilion_in_Gyeongbokgung_Palace.jpg -->

entries.name: Hwaseong Fortress
entries.desc: A late-Joseon fortress encircling the city of Suwon, built under King Jeongjo. Its walls, gates, and command posts blend Korean design with contemporary military engineering.

photo.alt: Stone walls of Hwaseong Fortress encircling Suwon
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hwaseong-fortress.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Hwaseong fortress walls.jpg" by Adil · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hwaseong_fortress_walls.jpg -->

entries.name: Alcázar of Segovia
entries.desc: A distinctive ship-shaped fortress rising on a rocky crag above the confluence of two rivers. It served as a royal palace and is often cited among the inspirations for Disney castles.

photo.alt: Ship-shaped Alcázar of Segovia rising above the river confluence
photo.credit: <!-- photo: alcazar-of-segovia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Panorámica Otoño Alcázar de Segovia.jpg" by Ángel Sanz de Andrés · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Panor%C3%A1mica_Oto%C3%B1o_Alc%C3%A1zar_de_Segovia.jpg -->

entries.name: Real Alcázar of Seville
entries.desc: The oldest royal palace still in use in Europe, built across a thousand years by Moorish, Gothic, and Renaissance rulers. Its 14th-century Mudéjar halls, geometric tilework, and walled gardens are among the finest anywhere.

photo.alt: Real Alcázar palace complex in Seville
photo.credit: <!-- photo: real-alcazar-of-seville.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Real Alcázar de Sevilla, Sevilla (42052074324).jpg" by FlyingCrimsonPig · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Real_Alc%C3%A1zar_de_Sevilla,_Sevilla_(42052074324).jpg -->

entries.name: Sigiriya
entries.desc: A 5th-century royal citadel and pleasure garden built atop an isolated granite column rising 200 m from the jungle. Its frescoed cave galleries, water gardens, and lion-paw gateway rank among the most dramatic fortress sites in Asia.

photo.alt: Sigiriya rock column rising from the surrounding jungle
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sigiriya-rock.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Sigiriya.jpg" by Bernard Gagnon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sigiriya.jpg -->

entries.name: Drottningholm Palace
entries.desc: A Baroque palace on an island in Lake Mälaren, the private residence of the Swedish royal family. Its grounds include a well-preserved 18th-century theatre and formal gardens.

photo.alt: Drottningholm Palace on its island in Lake Mälaren
photo.credit: <!-- photo: drottningholm-palace.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Drottningholm Palace (by Pudelek) 1.jpg" by Pudelek · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Drottningholm_Palace_(by_Pudelek)_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Château de Chillon
entries.desc: An island castle on the shore of Lake Geneva, long controlling the road along the lake. Lord Byron's poem The Prisoner of Chillon made its dungeon famous.

photo.alt: Château de Chillon on the shore of Lake Geneva
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chateau-de-chillon.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Château de Chillon et Dents du Midi.jpg" by Christian David · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Chillon_et_Dents_du_Midi.jpg -->

entries.name: Citadel of Aleppo
entries.desc: A great fortified palace atop a massive mound in the old city of Aleppo, one of the oldest and largest castles in the world. Its site has been occupied since ancient times.

photo.alt: The Citadel of Aleppo atop its fortified mound in the old city
photo.credit: <!-- photo: citadel-of-aleppo.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Citadel of Aleppo.jpg" by Memorino · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Citadel_of_Aleppo.jpg -->

entries.name: Krak des Chevaliers
entries.desc: A Crusader castle held by the Knights Hospitaller, widely regarded as one of the best-preserved medieval castles in the world. Its concentric walls set a benchmark in military design.

photo.alt: Krak des Chevaliers Crusader castle with its concentric fortified walls
photo.credit: <!-- photo: krak-des-chevaliers.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Krak des Chevaliers 01.jpg" by Bernard Gagnon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Krak_des_Chevaliers_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Topkapi Palace
entries.desc: The main residence and administrative court of the Ottoman sultans for roughly four centuries. Now a museum, it displays imperial treasures, the harem, and sacred relics.

photo.alt: Gate of Salutation entrance towers at Topkapi Palace, Istanbul
photo.credit: <!-- photo: topkapi-palace.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Topkapi Palace (Topkapı Sarayı), Istanbul, Turkey" by l0da_ralta · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Topkapi_Palace_(Topkap%C4%B1_Saray%C4%B1),_Istanbul,_Turkey_(9603571659).jpg -->

meta: Nearly 400 Years as Seat of Power

entries.name: Balmoral Castle
entries.desc: A Scottish Baronial estate in the Highlands, purchased and rebuilt under Queen Victoria and Prince Albert. It remains a private residence of the British royal family.

photo.alt: Balmoral Castle, the royal family's Highland estate in Aberdeenshire
photo.credit: <!-- photo: balmoral-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Balmoral Castle, Scotland.jpg" by BK59 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Balmoral_Castle,_Scotland.jpg -->

entries.name: Bodiam Castle
entries.desc: A 14th-century moated castle built by a former knight of Edward III. Its symmetrical outer walls surrounded by a broad water moat make it a classic image of the medieval fortress.

photo.alt: Bodiam Castle's moated walls in the East Sussex countryside
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bodiam-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Bodiam Castle south.jpg" by Phillip Capper · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bodiam_Castle_south.jpg -->

entries.name: Conwy Castle
entries.desc: A medieval fortress built by Edward I during his conquest of Wales, part of a ring of castles ringing the region. It is considered one of the finest examples of Edwardian military architecture.

photo.alt: Conwy Castle's medieval towers overlooking the town in North Wales
photo.credit: <!-- photo: conwy-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Conwy Castle.jpg" by Dbenbenn (assumed) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Conwy_Castle.jpg -->

entries.name: Edinburgh Castle
entries.desc: A historic fortress perched on Castle Rock, an extinct volcanic plug dominating the city skyline. It houses the Scottish Crown Jewels and the Stone of Destiny.

photo.alt: Edinburgh Castle atop Castle Rock overlooking the city
photo.credit: <!-- photo: edinburgh-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Edinburgh Castle from the North.JPG" by Kim Traynor · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Edinburgh_Castle_from_the_North.JPG -->

entries.name: Eilean Donan Castle
entries.desc: A Highland castle on a small tidal island where three sea lochs meet, reached by a stone footbridge. Ruined in the 18th century, it was fully reconstructed in the early 1900s.

photo.alt: Eilean Donan Castle on its tidal island, reached by a stone footbridge
photo.credit: <!-- photo: eilean-donan-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Eilean Donan Castle, Scotland - Jan 2011.jpg" by Diliff · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Eilean_Donan_Castle,_Scotland_-_Jan_2011.jpg -->

entries.name: Stirling Castle
entries.desc: A commanding fortress on a volcanic crag that guarded the strategic gateway to the Scottish Highlands. It was a favoured residence of the Stewart monarchs and a coronation site.

photo.alt: Stirling Castle on its volcanic crag
photo.credit: <!-- photo: stirling-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "2018 07 07 Burg Sterling" by Bewahrerderwerte · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:2018_07_07_Burg_Sterling.jpg -->

entries.name: Tower of London
entries.desc: A riverside fortress begun under William the Conqueror that has served as royal palace, prison, and armoury. It guards the Crown Jewels and is watched by the Yeoman Warders.

photo.alt: Tower of London viewed from the River Thames
photo.credit: <!-- photo: tower-of-london.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Tower of London viewed from the River Thames" by Bob Collowan · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Tower_of_London_viewed_from_the_River_Thames.jpg -->

entries.name: Urquhart Castle
entries.desc: A ruined hilltop castle on the western shore of Loch Ness, once one of the largest in Scotland. Demolished in 1692 to prevent Jacobite use, its five-storey tower keep is the most photographed castle ruin in Scotland.

photo.alt: Ruined tower keep of Urquhart Castle beside Loch Ness
photo.credit: <!-- photo: urquhart-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Castle Urquhart and Loch Ness - geograph.org.uk - 2303633" by David Dixon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castle_Urquhart_and_Loch_Ness_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2303633.jpg -->

entries.name: Warwick Castle
entries.desc: A medieval fortress on a bend of the River Avon, developed from an earthwork raised by William the Conqueror. It now operates as a visitor attraction with restored ramparts and towers.

photo.alt: Warwick Castle across the River Avon
photo.credit: <!-- photo: warwick-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Exterior of Warwick Castle from across the River Avon, 2009" by Haydn Curtis · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Exterior_of_Warwick_Castle_from_across_the_River_Avon,_2009.jpg -->

entries.name: Windsor Castle
entries.desc: Founded by William the Conqueror, it is the oldest and largest inhabited castle in the world and a working royal residence of the British monarch.

photo.alt: Windsor Castle exterior
photo.credit: <!-- photo: windsor-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Exterior of Windsor Castle" by Rachelle Haun · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Exterior_of_Windsor_Castle.jpg -->

meta: Oldest Inhabited Castle in the World

entries.name: Castillo de San Marcos
entries.desc: The oldest masonry fort in the continental United States, built by the Spanish to defend St. Augustine. Its walls are made of coquina, a soft shell-stone that absorbed cannon fire.

photo.alt: Castillo de San Marcos coquina fort walls
photo.credit: <!-- photo: castillo-de-san-marcos.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Castillo de San Marcos National Monument St Augustine, FL" by Mac Applethorpe · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Castillo_de_San_Marcos_National_Monument_St_Augustine,_FL.jpg -->

meta: Oldest Masonry Fort in the Continental US

entries.name: Castillo San Felipe del Morro
entries.desc: A Spanish colonial citadel guarding the entrance to San Juan Bay, built in stages to defend the harbour from sea attack. Its multi-level bastions rise above the Atlantic.

photo.alt: Castillo San Felipe del Morro overlooking the Atlantic
photo.credit: <!-- photo: castillo-san-felipe-del-morro.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Beautiful Puerto Rico Castillo San Felipe del Morro - Fuerte San Felipe del Morro" by Memasmuffn · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Beautiful_Puerto_Rico_Castillo_San_Felipe_del_Morro_-_Fuerte_San_Felipe_del_Morro.jpg -->
